summary: Charity argues that engineering managers are essential even at startups because hierarchy is a property of self-organizing systems that reduces coordination costs and information overload. She makes the case through systems theory (managers coordinate between teams and reduce context switching) and through pragmatism (most orgs have managers because the model is well-understood, and manager-free experiments have repeatedly failed).
key_points: Hierarchy is not intrinsically authoritarian — it's a property of self-organizing systems that emerges to reduce coordination costs and information overload
The 'socio' and 'technical' in sociotechnical systems are inseparable; most engineering work is glue work drawing on both skill sets
Engineering managers serve as useful abstractions — centralizing coordination into one role massively reduces context switching for engineers
Manager-free org experiments (holacracy at Medium/GitHub, etc.) have all been quietly abandoned due to chaos and poor execution
More lines of code does not equal more productivity — customers care about solving problems, not code output
money_quotes: When there is no explicit structure or hierarchy, the result is not freedom and egalitarianism, it's 'informal, unacknowledged, and unaccountable leadership.'
If you aren't making building the organization someone's number one job, it won't be anyone's number one job, which means it probably won't get done very well.
Your customers give zero fucks how many lines of code you write. They care about whether you are building the right things and solving problems that matter to them.
In natural hierarchies, we look up for purpose and down for function.
The nature of glue work is such that it too-easily expands to consume all available time and space. Constraints are good. Feeling a bit underresourced is good, and should be the norm.
key_insight: Engineering managers are not overhead but a necessary abstraction layer in self-organizing systems — they reduce coordination costs, prevent information overload, and ensure that building the organization is someone's primary job rather than no one's.
